problem_statement: LLMs used for code and natural-language tasks are vulnerable to adversarial attacks that bypass guardrails and produce harmful or vulnerable code, and developers lack a structured way to reason about and demonstrate compliant adversarial robustness under regulations like the EU AI Act.
magnitude_evidence: At least 20 identifiable duties concern the (cyber)security, safety and robustness of AI systems under the EU AI Act (ref [16]); Article 15 Paragraph 5 explicitly demands protection from adversarial attacks
insufficiency_evidence: "the success rate of simple variations of known attack patterns shows that relying purely on LLMs is not enough to claim robustness"; also "we posit that successful attacks will occur" and guardrail coverage "is not easily known beforehand"
solution_mechanisms: meta-layer for dynamic risk management and reasoning/reporting
sandbox verification and confidence signals for users
key_constructs: assurance case for compliant adversarial robustness
Swiss cheese diagram of LLM layers
de facto incident vs. serious incident
